finding_text: CDC states symptoms typically begin about 2 days after infection, with a range of 1-4 days
codeblue_translation: Use a short default lookback window for exposure reconstruction and daily dynamic risk refresh

finding_text: CDC states influenza viruses can be detected beginning one day before symptoms develop
codeblue_translation: Begin transmission-risk logic before symptom onset, not only after recognition

finding_text: CDC states influenza can be detected up to five to seven days after becoming sick
codeblue_translation: Use 5-7 days as the usual infectious window for default inpatient logic

finding_text: CDC states people with flu are most contagious during the first three days of illness
codeblue_translation: Weight early illness as the highest transmission-risk window

finding_text: CDC states some people, including young children and those with weakened immune systems, may be contagious longer
codeblue_translation: Route prolonged-course review rather than using a rigid stop date in these groups

finding_text: CDC recommends Droplet Precautions for 7 days after illness onset or until 24 hours after fever and respiratory symptoms resolve, whichever is longer
codeblue_translation: Use this as the default inpatient precaution-duration rule

finding_text: CDC says facilities may choose longer Droplet Precautions for young children or severely immunocompromised patients
codeblue_translation: Allow extension beyond the default rule when prolonged-shedding risk is high

finding_text: The pack should support daily recalculation of patient, staff, and ward influenza logic
codeblue_translation: Use a 24-hour refresh cadence for dynamic influenza reasoning
confidence_note: Architecture rule rather than biologic evidence

finding_text: Brazil guidance states influenza is identified throughout the year in Brazil with regional circulation differences
codeblue_translation: Never use off-season timing alone to rule out influenza
confidence_note: Strong official support for deployment design
